content:
7 min read
2 hours ago

--

Hi, I’m Junseong Jeon, a Software Engineer on the HandyBus team — a fandom-focused, demand-responsive shuttle service.
In this post, I want to share how I worked through resolving a race condition between WebView navigation and the page lifecycle that I discovered while tracking Push Notification Opens in a WebView environment.
What made this especially tricky was that `fetch()`’s `Load Failed` response wasn’t a well-defined error like a 4xx or 5xx — so pinning down the exact cause was hard. I got to the answer by forming several hypotheses and testing them one by one, narrowing things down step by step.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

1. The Problem
To track when users open push notifications, I was passing the `notificationDeliveryId` received from the push notification into the WebView, then sending it to a POST API via `fetch()`.
The problem was that when a user tapped a DeepLink-type push notification from a Terminated state, the Push Notification Open tracking failed.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

That meant one of two steps was failing: (1) app → web, or (2) web → backend server. Adding logs, here’s what I found:
(1) App → Web
- Confirmed that `notificationDeliveryId` was sent to the WebView via the message handler.
(2) Web → Backend Server
- Confirmed the listener was invoked in `GlobalWebviewListener`.
- Confirmed the notification event POST API call was made.
- Confirmed the POST API response came back as `Load Failed`.
It was really strange. I was using the fetch API and had never seen a response like this. So I needed to dig into what this error actually was — which I’ll unpack below.
2. fetch response: Load Failed
Logging inside the WebView showed that the notification event POST API’s response was neither a 4xx nor 5xx error, but `Load Failed`. Looking into it, I found the following clues.
According to the fetch standard docs, the spec for the `fetch()` method states that “in the case of a network error, the promise is rejected with a `TypeError`.”
// fetch standard 5.6. Fetch methods If response is a network error,
then reject p with a TypeError and abort these steps.

• Browsers display `TypeError` with different messages. iOS WebKit/Safari shows it as `Load Failed` ← which exactly matches the error we were experiencing.
But if we assume it’s a network error — during testing, all the other API requests worked fine, so why was a network error occurring intermittently only in this one spot? Let’s take a closer look at how a network error is defined.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

According to W3C Network Error Logging, the predefined network error types include redirects under the “Transmission of request and response errors” category.
In other words, if a fetch is interrupted by a page lifecycle transition such as `pagehide` (e.g., due to navigation), that’s also treated as a network error.
2–1. Looking at It Through the Page Lifecycle Lens
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A browser page doesn’t operate at the simple level of “the page is visible / not visible” — it operates with lifecycle states.
Representative events include:
- visibilitychange
- pagehide
- beforeunload
- unload (modern browsers trend away from this, since it interferes with the back/forward cache)
When navigation begins, the existing page enters a page lifecycle transition through events like `visibilitychange(hidden)` and `pagehide`.
During this process, the existing JavaScript execution context and in-flight network tasks can be cleaned up. Since an ordinary fetch request is bound to the current page’s execution context, it can be interrupted during a lifecycle transition.
To pin this down precisely, I logged the WebView’s state. Let’s compare the logs for a success case and a failure case, both from the Terminated state.
// Success case logsloadStart url="/"
loadEnd url="/"
processPendingDeepLink
handleDeepLink:injectJavaScript finalPath="/mypage"
pendingNotificationDeliveryId remainsloadEnd url="/mypage"
processPendingNotification
sendNotificationOpened <-- notification event post api
Message sent to web: NOTIFICATION_OPENEDweb pageUrl="/mypage"Notification open event received / sending started
Notification open event sent successfully
// 실패 케이스 로그loadEnd url="/"
processPendingDeepLink
handleDeepLink:injectJavaScript finalPath="/mypage"
pendingNotificationDeliveryId remainsloadStart url="/mypage"
loadEnd url="/" <-- late loadEnd from previous page
processPendingNotification
sendNotificationOpened <-- notification event post api
Message sent to web: NOTIFICATION_OPENEDweb pageUrl="/"
Notification open event received / sending started
Notification open event send failed
errorMessage: "Load failed"pagehide pageUrl="/"
visibilitychange hidden pageUrl="/"loadEnd url="/mypage"Analyzing the logs gives us the following. This shows that the page lifecycle transition began before the notification API request completed.
- Success: The API request happened *after* navigation to the `/mypage` route completed.
- Failure: The API request happened on the `/` route, and then navigation to `/mypage` occurred.
Ultimately, the essence of the problem was a timing race in which the page lifecycle transition occurred before the fetch completion.
3. The Solution
Once the page lifecycle enters the terminated stage, the existing JavaScript execution context is cleaned up, and the in-flight fetch request can be interrupted along with it. I had several possible solutions to choose from.
To guarantee that the page lifecycle transition (`pagehide → hidden`) doesn’t occur until the API request completes, I could consider: (1) proceeding with navigation *after* the API request completes, or (2) making the API request *after* navigation proceeds.
(1) Proceed with navigation after the API request completes requires additional design for a mechanism to detect API success. And if you also account for a fallback — setting a 1–3 second timeout and retrying the API after navigation if it’s exceeded — the design can balloon in complexity.
(2) Make the API request after navigation proceeds is vulnerable to user intervention. If the user goes back or kills the app before navigation completes, the API call can still fail.
Both approaches had clear drawbacks, so I had to look for another way. The key is to make navigation and the POST API not affect each other. With that in mind, my first thought was to use a Worker running on a separate thread to offload the POST API onto a background thread.
(3) A Worker is restricted from DOM access but can handle requests like fetch. However, since a Worker isn’t completely independent from the current page context either, I judged it would be hard to reliably guarantee request completion during the page lifecycle transition (`pagehide → hidden`).
(4) sendBeacon() was another candidate. This API is designed to asynchronously send small POST requests of an analytics/logging nature, even when the page is unloading or moving to the next navigation. However, it can’t use custom headers, so I couldn’t reuse the existing authentication structure as-is — especially for Authorization Bearer token–based APIs.
(5) The method I ultimately chose was to leverage fetch’s `keepalive` option. It can keep sending a small network request even through the page lifecycle transition (`pagehide → hidden`), and it let me keep the existing `fetch()`-based API call structure with a clear, well-defined shape.
fetch(..., { keepalive: true })
4. A Problem That Remained Even After keepalive
After applying keepalive, most cases were resolved — but one phenomenon remained: after the app had gone unused for a certain period, only the first request would fail again.
Since the access token’s expiration time was about an hour, I began to suspect the auth refresh flow.
Tracing it, the problem turned out to be not the notification tracking API itself, but the auth-recovery logic in the shared auth instance.
The existing authInstance had a flow like:
- Refresh the token when the access token expires
- On failure, log out and redirect to login
- Re-run the retry request
But notification tracking was an analytics-type request, and it didn’t need to be tightly coupled to the auth refresh / redirect flow.
In particular, in the situation where even the refresh token had expired, additional design could become necessary:
- Replaying pending tracking after login completes
- Preventing duplicates
- Queue management
And if the user gave up on logging in, there was a possibility that the tracking itself would be permanently lost.
In the end, I resolved this by separating the notification open tracking API onto a dedicated instance with no auth dependency, instead of authInstance.
Since the notificationDeliveryId itself is issued uniquely to each user during the push delivery process, I judged that this endpoint could be tracked reliably even without separate authentication.
5. Wrapping Up
The biggest thing I felt while tracing this issue is that network requests in a browser environment aren’t a problem that ends with just a fetch() call.
Different layers — like the Page Lifecycle and auth-recovery logic — were entangled far more tightly than expected, and as a result, even a simple tracking API request could fail depending on timing.
At first I thought it was a plain network error, but the real cause was a race condition in which the request is interrupted during the page lifecycle transition, combined with an auth recovery flow that was excessively coupled to an analytics request.
Through this experience, I once again felt firsthand that the very act of “sending a request” is an asynchronous system operating on top of the browser lifecycle. In particular, I learned that requests where deliverability matters more than guaranteed success — like tracking or logging — should be designed from a different perspective than ordinary API requests.
I hope this post can be a small hint for others facing a similar problem as they work to narrow down the cause.
Reference
- https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#dom-global-fetch
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/78430384/is-there-a-standard-way-to-handle-network-errors-and-blocked-requests-across-bro
- https://www.w3.org/TR/network-error-logging/?utm_source=chatgpt.com#predefined-network-error-types
- https://developer.chrome.com/docs/web-platform/page-lifecycle-api
